abstract:
We investigate the optimal model size and number of tokens for training a
transformer language model under a given compute budget. We find that current
large language models are significantly undertrained, a consequence of the
recent focus on scaling language models whilst keeping the amount of training
data constant. By training over 400 language models ranging from 70 million to
over 16 billion parameters on 5 to 500 billion tokens, we find that for
compute-optimal training, the model size and the number of training tokens
should be scaled equally: for every doubling of model size the number of
training tokens should also be doubled. We test this hypothesis by training a
predicted compute-optimal model, Chinchilla, that uses the same compute budget
as Gopher but with 70B parameters and 4$\times$ more more data. Chinchilla
uniformly and significantly outperforms Gopher (280B), GPT-3 (175B), Jurassic-1
(178B), and Megatron-Turing NLG (530B) on a large range of downstream
evaluation tasks. This also means that Chinchilla uses substantially less
compute for fine-tuning and inference, greatly facilitating downstream usage.
As a highlight, Chinchilla reaches a state-of-the-art average accuracy of 67.5%
on the MMLU benchmark, greater than a 7% improvement over Gopher.

keyInsights: For compute-optimal training, model size and training tokens should be scaled equally, meaning both should be doubled for every doubling of compute.
A precise mathematical relationship was derived: N_optimal "propto" C^(0.5) and D_optimal ":propto" C^(0.5), where N is model size, D is dataset size, and C is compute budget.
Prior large language models like GPT-3 and Gopher are significantly under-trained, utilizing approximately 5-10 times fewer tokens than optimal for their parameter count.
originalProblem: Current large language models are significantly under-trained relative to their parameter count, leading to suboptimal performance for given computational budgets.
Previous scaling laws, like those from Kaplan et al. (2020), primarily emphasized increasing model size over data size, potentially misleading resource allocation.
The imbalance between model size and training data results in unnecessarily large and computationally expensive models that do not achieve their full potential.
results: The 70B parameter Chinchilla model, trained optimally on 1.4 trillion tokens, achieved significantly lower perplexity and consistently outperformed larger models such as Gopher (280B parameters) and GPT-3 (175B parameters).
Chinchilla achieved a state-of-the-art average accuracy of 67.5% on the Massive Multitask Language Understanding (MMLU) benchmark, surpassing Gopher (60%) and GPT-3 (43.9%).
The inference cost of Chinchilla is substantially lower than that of larger models, enabling more efficient deployment and fine-tuning while reducing the carbon footprint of AI.
solution: Conducted an extensive empirical study by training over 400 transformer language models with parameter counts from 70 million to 16 billion on varying amounts of data (5 billion to over 500 billion tokens).
Employed three complementary analysis approaches: fixing model size and varying data, analyzing isoFLOP profiles, and fitting a parametric loss function to empirical data.

Identified the optimal amount of training data that minimized loss for a given compute budget for each model size, precisely mapping the relationship between model size, training tokens, and computational resources.
paper_summary.summary: Google DeepMind research establishes new compute-optimal scaling laws for large language models, demonstrating that model size and training data should scale equally for efficient training. The resulting 70B parameter Chinchilla model, trained on 1.4 trillion tokens, achieved lower perplexity and outperformed larger models like Gopher and GPT-3 on various benchmarks, validating the new scaling approach.

title: Training Compute-Optimal Large Language Models

abstract: Scaling laws are a critical component of the LLM development pipeline, most famously as a way to forecast training decisions such as 'compute-optimally' trading-off parameter count and dataset size, alongside a more recent growing list of other crucial decisions. In this work, we ask whether compute-optimal scaling behaviour can be skill-dependent. In particular, we examine knowledge and reasoning-based skills such as knowledge-based QA and code generation, and we answer this question in the affirmative: scaling laws are skill-dependent. Next, to understand whether skill-dependent scaling is an artefact of the pretraining datamix, we conduct an extensive ablation of different datamixes and find that, also when correcting for datamix differences, knowledge and code exhibit fundamental differences in scaling behaviour. We conclude with an analysis of how our findings relate to standard compute-optimal scaling using a validation set, and find that a misspecified validation set can impact compute-optimal parameter count by nearly 50%, depending on its skill composition.

keyInsights: Compute-optimal scaling behavior is skill-dependent: knowledge-based QA is intrinsically more "capacity-hungry" (benefits from more parameters), while code generation is more "data-hungry" (benefits from more training tokens).

This skill-dependent scaling is fundamental rather than merely an artifact of the pretraining datamix, as knowledge QA remained substantially more capacity-hungry than code even when data proportions were controlled.

Strategic adjustment of pretraining data mixtures can align skill-specific compute optima, and the choice of validation set critically impacts the estimated optimal parameter count, requiring careful alignment with target skills.
originalProblem: Traditional neural scaling laws determine compute-optimal (CO) parameter-data trade-offs based on aggregate model performance, such as overall negative log-likelihood.

It was unclear if these aggregate COs are universally optimal for diverse individual LLM skills like knowledge retrieval or reasoning, or if different skills have distinct requirements for model capacity versus data volume.
Without a skill-aware understanding of COs, LLM pretraining strategies risk inefficient resource allocation and suboptimal performance for targeted capabilities.
results: Under a canonical datamix, knowledge-based QA consistently showed capacity-hungry behavior and code generation exhibited data-hungry behavior across 19 diverse evaluation datasets.

Increasing skill-relevant data in the pretraining mix shifted skill-specific COs, and an optimal ratio of approximately 2.1 times more code data than knowledge data was found to align their parameter optima at the smallest compute scale.

The estimated optimal parameter count could vary by nearly 50% depending on the datamix and over 30% across different validation sets at smaller scales, demonstrating the significant influence of validation set composition on optimal model sizing.
solution: Trained numerous Transformer-based LLMs (40M to 8B parameters) across nine compute scales (6 × 10^18 to 3 × 10^21 FLOPs) following the Llama 3 training methodology.

Determined compute optima for specific skills (knowledge-based QA and code generation) by optimizing Negative Log-Likelihood (NLL) on respective skill datasets, comparing these to aggregate performance optima.

Conducted extensive ablations on pretraining datamixes by varying knowledge and code data proportions to understand their influence on skill-dependent COs, and analyzed the impact of different validation sets on estimated optimal parameter counts.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from Meta and the University of Wisconsin investigated compute-optimal scaling for large language models, finding that the optimal trade-off between model parameters and training data tokens is skill-dependent. They demonstrated that knowledge-based tasks are "capacity-hungry" while code generation is "data-hungry," providing empirical strategies for aligning these optima through data mixture adjustments and highlighting the critical role of validation set composition.

title: Compute Optimal Scaling of Skills: Knowledge vs Reasoning

abstract: We rethink test-time scaling laws from a practical efficiency perspective, revealing that the effectiveness of smaller models is significantly overestimated. Prior work, grounded in compute-optimality, overlooks critical memory access bottlenecks introduced by inference-time strategies (e.g., Best-of-$N$, long CoTs). Our holistic analysis, spanning models from 0.6B to 32B parameters, reveals a new Kinetics Scaling Law that better guides resource allocation by incorporating both computation and memory access costs. Kinetics Scaling Law suggests that test-time compute is more effective when used on models above a threshold than smaller ones. A key reason is that in TTS, attention, rather than parameter count, emerges as the dominant cost factor. Motivated by this, we propose a new scaling paradigm centered on sparse attention, which lowers per-token cost and enables longer generations and more parallel samples within the same resource budget. Empirically, we show that sparse attention models consistently outperform dense counterparts, achieving over 60 points gains in low-cost regimes and over 5 points gains in high-cost regimes for problem-solving accuracy on AIME, encompassing evaluations on state-of-the-art MoEs. These results suggest that sparse attention is essential and increasingly important with more computing invested, for realizing the full potential of test-time scaling where, unlike training, accuracy has yet to saturate as a function of computation, and continues to improve through increased generation. The code is available at this https URL.

keyInsights: Existing FLOPs-centric test-time scaling laws are incomplete, as memory access costs (especially for the KV cache) are often the dominant bottleneck during LLM inference, particularly for long sequences.

Smaller models are significantly less efficient than previously thought in test-time scenarios due to disproportionately higher KV memory costs relative to their parameter size, shifting optimal resource allocation towards larger models or sparse attention.

The cost model for test-time scaling shifts from linear to quadratic with generation length when attention costs, driven by KV cache access, become dominant, making long generations prohibitively expensive for dense models.
originalProblem: Prior test-time scaling laws for Large Language Models (LLMs) primarily focused on FLOPs, neglecting memory access costs which are a critical bottleneck in real-world inference.

This FLOPs-centric view led to an overestimation of the effectiveness of smaller models and suboptimal resource allocation for advanced test-time strategies like Chain-of-Thought and Best-of-N sampling.

The quadratic growth of attention costs with sequence length, particularly due to Key-Value (KV) cache access, was not adequately addressed, limiting the practical scalability of long-sequence generation.
results:
The Kinetics scaling law demonstrates up to a 3
reduction in resource demands to achieve the same accuracy on NVIDIA B200 hardware compared to FLOPs-only guided allocation.

Sparse attention models achieve up to 60 percentage points higher problem-solving accuracy in low-cost regimes and require over 10
ційних.

A practical implementation of block top-k attention yields a 45-point accuracy gain in the low-cost regime and substantially improves inference throughput (e.g., 25.9-26.2
for Qwen3-0.6B) compared to dense attention.
solution: Introduces the "Kinetics Scaling Law," a new cost model that explicitly incorporates both computation (FLOPs) and memory access costs (dominated by KV cache) using an "equivalent FLOPs" (eFLOPs) metric.
Conducts an Iso-Cost analysis to re-evaluate optimal resource allocation between model size and test-time strategies, revealing a critical model size threshold for efficient strategy application.

Proposes "Sparse Kinetics," a new scaling paradigm centered on sparse attention mechanisms, which transforms the quadratic attention cost into a linear one, significantly improving efficiency and making smaller models more viable.
paper_summary.summary: This work introduces the "Kinetics Scaling Law," a new cost model for Large Language Model inference that integrates both computation and memory access costs, especially Key-Value cache access. The paper demonstrates that previous FLOPs-centric scaling laws significantly overestimate the efficiency of smaller models in test-time scenarios and proposes sparse attention as an architectural solution to achieve substantial gains in problem-solving accuracy and throughput.

title: Kinetics: Rethinking Test-Time Scaling Laws

abstract:
While the scaling laws of large language models (LLMs) training have been
extensively studied, optimal inference configurations of LLMs remain
underexplored. We study inference scaling laws (aka test-time scaling laws) and
compute-optimal inference, focusing on the trade-offs between model sizes and
generating additional tokens with different inference strategies. As a first
step towards understanding and designing compute-optimal inference methods, we
studied cost-performance trade-offs for inference strategies such as greedy
search, majority voting, best-of-$n$, weighted voting, and two different tree
search algorithms, using different model sizes and compute budgets. Our
findings suggest that scaling inference compute with inference strategies can
be more computationally efficient than scaling model parameters. Additionally,
smaller models combined with advanced inference algorithms offer Pareto-optimal
trade-offs in cost and performance. For example, the Llemma-7B model, when
paired with our novel tree search algorithm, consistently outperforms the
Llemma-34B model across all tested inference strategies on the MATH benchmark.
We hope these insights contribute to a deeper understanding of inference
scaling laws (test-time scaling laws) for LLMs.

keyInsights: Optimal model size for LLM inference is not static but varies with the available compute budget, suggesting that smaller models paired with effective inference strategies can be compute-optimal at lower to moderate budgets.

Sampling and voting-based inference strategies exhibit theoretical limits, asymptotically saturating in accuracy due to the underlying language model's output probabilities, necessitating more advanced algorithmic approaches for higher performance.

Advanced inference algorithms, particularly the proposed REBASE, offer superior cost-performance trade-offs by effectively leveraging tree search principles without the high computational overhead of traditional methods like MCTS.
originalProblem: Prior scaling law research for Large Language Models (LLMs) predominantly focused on the training phase, leaving inference-time scaling laws and compute-optimal deployment strategies largely unexplored.
There was a lack of systematic understanding regarding the trade-offs between performance gains and computational costs associated with various LLM inference techniques.

Existing inference strategies, such as simple sampling and Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS), faced limitations; sampling methods exhibit diminishing returns and saturation, while MCTS often proves computationally inefficient due to costly rollouts.
results: Smaller models (e.g., Llemma-7B) combined with advanced inference strategies (like REBASE) achieved comparable or superior accuracy to significantly larger models (e.g., Llemma-34B) while consuming approximately 2x less total FLOPs.

REBASE consistently demonstrated Pareto-optimal cost-performance across all tested models, tasks, and compute budgets, outperforming both sampling-based methods and MCTS, which was found to be less efficient due to high rollout costs.

REBASE provided greater performance gains on harder mathematical reasoning problems (MATH levels 3-5) compared to easier ones, indicating its particular effectiveness for challenging tasks requiring complex reasoning.
solution: The authors formally defined and empirically investigated the compute-optimal inference problem, analyzing how LLM problem-solving performance scales with inference-time computational resources.

A comprehensive set of inference strategies, including sampling-based methods (greedy search, best-of-n, majority voting, weighted majority voting) and tree search algorithms (MCTS), were systematically evaluated for their cost-performance trade-offs.

A novel compute-optimal tree search algorithm, REBASE (REward BAlanced SEarch), was proposed, which uses a node-quality reward model to guide expansion, thereby eliminating the need for costly explicit rollouts inherent in MCTS.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from Tsinghua University and Carnegie Mellon University empirically analyzed inference scaling laws for large language models (LLMs) and introduced REBASE, a novel tree search algorithm. Their work demonstrates that smaller LLMs, when combined with compute-optimal inference strategies like REBASE, can achieve problem-solving performance comparable to or better than much larger models, with significantly reduced computational overhead during deployment.

title: Inference Scaling Laws: An Empirical Analysis of Compute-Optimal  Inference for Problem-Solving with Language Models

abstract: Recent progress in large language models (LLMs) highlights the power of scaling test-time compute to achieve strong performance on complex tasks, such as mathematical reasoning and code generation. This raises a critical question: how should model training be modified to optimize performance under a subsequent test-time compute strategy and budget? To explore this, we focus on pass@N, a simple test-time strategy that searches for a correct answer in $N$ independent samples. We show, surprisingly, that training with cross-entropy (CE) loss can be ${\it misaligned}$ with pass@N in that pass@N accuracy ${\it decreases}$ with longer training. We explain the origins of this misalignment in terms of model overconfidence induced by CE, and experimentally verify our prediction of overconfidence as an impediment to scaling test-time compute via pass@N. Furthermore we suggest a principled, modified training loss that is better aligned to pass@N by limiting model confidence and rescuing pass@N test performance. Our algorithm demonstrates improved mathematical reasoning on MATH and MiniF2F benchmarks under several scenarios: (1) providing answers to math questions; and (2) proving theorems by searching over proof trees of varying shapes. Overall our work underscores the importance of co-designing two traditionally separate phases of LLM development: training-time protocols and test-time search and reasoning strategies.

keyInsights: Standard cross-entropy loss, while beneficial for `pass@1`, induces model overconfidence that actively degrades `pass@N` performance by reducing solution diversity and exploration.
Optimal performance for scaled test-time compute (`pass@N`) requires a co-design approach where training explicitly accounts for the target inference strategy and budget.
The problem of overconfidence, detrimental to `pass@N`, is not unique to SFT and can also affect advanced reinforcement learning (RL) methods like GRPO.
originalProblem: Standard supervised fine-tuning (SFT) using cross-entropy (CE) loss leads to a paradoxical decrease in `pass@N` accuracy for complex reasoning tasks with longer training, especially for large N.
The training of Large Language Models (LLMs) is typically decoupled from specific test-time inference strategies and compute budgets, hindering optimal performance when scaling test-time compute.
Models trained with CE loss become overconfident in their greedy predictions, limiting the exploration of diverse, potentially correct solutions crucial for multi-sample inference (`pass@N`).
results: DCO loss consistently improves `pass@N` coverage over CE loss for direct answer generation on MATH and AIME24 datasets, achieving a Pareto-optimal performance frontier when the DCO training parameter N' aligns with test-time N.

A step-wise DCO ($DCO_{step}$) for theorem proving on LeanDojo significantly improves proof success rates, with ensembling models achieving gains (e.g., 5.2% on Mathlib, 4.1% on MiniF2F over CE baseline).
An approximate DCO ($DCO_a$) for Chain-of-Thought reasoning mitigates overconfidence and achieves superior `pass@N` performance compared to both CE loss and GRPO, especially for larger N values.
solution: Proposed Direct Coverage Optimization (DCO) loss, which directly optimizes the probability of finding a correct answer within N samples (`pass@N`).
Derived DCO's gradient, showing it incorporates an "overconfidence regularization factor" that attenuates gradients for examples where the model is already confident, thereby limiting overconfidence.
Introduced extensions like step-wise DCO for structured search (e.g., theorem proving) and approximate DCO for Chain-of-Thought reasoning using Monte Carlo estimation.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from Stanford University identify that standard fine-tuning with cross-entropy loss induces model overconfidence, which paradoxically degrades Large Language Model (LLM) performance when scaling test-time compute for mathematical reasoning. They propose Direct Coverage Optimization (DCO) loss, a novel training objective that limits overconfidence and significantly improves `pass@N` accuracy across various complex reasoning benchmarks.

title: Rethinking Fine-Tuning when Scaling Test-Time Compute: Limiting Confidence Improves Mathematical Reasoning

abstract: Low precision training and inference affect both the quality and cost of language models, but current scaling laws do not account for this. In this work, we devise "precision-aware" scaling laws for both training and inference. We propose that training in lower precision reduces the model's "effective parameter count," allowing us to predict the additional loss incurred from training in low precision and post-train quantization. For inference, we find that the degradation introduced by post-training quantization increases as models are trained on more data, eventually making additional pretraining data actively harmful. For training, our scaling laws allow us to predict the loss of a model with different parts in different precisions, and suggest that training larger models in lower precision may be compute optimal. We unify the scaling laws for post and pretraining quantization to arrive at a single functional form that predicts degradation from training and inference in varied precisions. We fit on over 465 pretraining runs and validate our predictions on model sizes up to 1.7B parameters trained on up to 26B tokens.

keyInsights: Numerical precision reduces a model's "effective parameter count," allowing for trade-offs between precision and model size to achieve similar performance.
Models trained on more data (overtrained models) exhibit increased susceptibility to performance degradation from post-training quantization.
Training models in lower precision can robustify them against subsequent post-training quantization, an effect that competes with the increased degradation seen in effectively smaller models.
originalProblem: Existing large language model (LLM) scaling laws did not account for the impact of numerical precision on model quality and training costs.
There was a lack of understanding regarding the quantitative trade-offs between precision, model parameters, and dataset size.
The distinct effects of precision during training (quantized training) versus inference (post-train quantization) were not integrated into a unified framework.
results: The compute-optimal pretraining precision for integer-type quantization is generally around 7-8 bits, often independent of the total compute budget.
Post-training quantization (PTQ) degradation increases significantly with the amount of pretraining data, indicating that for PTQ, excessive data can be detrimental to performance.

A unified scaling law was developed that accurately predicts LLM loss across varied training and inference precisions, parameter counts, and data budgets, demonstrating a strong fit to over 1000 experimental data points.
solution: Conducted over 465 pretraining runs on Transformer++ models, systematically varying model sizes (30M-220M), data tokens (1.5B-26B), and precision (3-16 bits) on the Dolma V1.7 dataset.
Introduced the concept of an "effective parameter count" (N_eff) to integrate the impact of training precision into established scaling law functional forms.

Developed and fitted novel functional forms, including a saturating exponential for N_eff and power laws for post-training quantization degradation, to model the observed multi-dimensional scaling behaviors.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from Harvard, Stanford, MIT, and Databricks developed unified scaling laws that integrate numerical precision with model and data scale. Their findings indicate that the compute-optimal training precision is often around 7-8 bits and that models trained on excessive data become more susceptible to performance degradation from post-training quantization.

title: Scaling Laws for Precision

abstract: Test-time scaling (TTS) -- the dynamic allocation of compute during inference -- is a promising direction for improving reasoning in large language models (LLMs). However, a systematic comparison of well-known TTS strategies under identical conditions is missing, and the influence of model type and problem difficulty on performance remains unclear. To address these gaps, we conduct the first large-scale study of TTS, spanning over thirty billion tokens generated using eight open-source LLMs (7B to 235B parameters), across four reasoning datasets. We observe three consistent trends: (1) no single TTS strategy universally dominates; (2) reasoning models exhibit distinct trace-quality patterns across problem difficulty and trace length, forming short-horizon and long-horizon categories; and (3) for a given model type, the optimal TTS performance scales monotonically with compute budget. Based on these insights, we provide a practical recipe for selecting the best TTS strategy, considering problem difficulty, model type, and compute budget, providing a practical guide to effective inference-time scaling.

keyInsights: No single TTS strategy universally outperforms others, and Beam Search consistently exhibited inverse or no scaling, where more compute did not improve or even degraded accuracy.

LLMs possess distinct "reasoning horizons," with 'short-horizon' models (e.g., DeepSeek-R1) favoring shorter traces and 'long-horizon' models (e.g., GPT-OSS-120B) benefiting from longer traces on harder problems.
The optimal TTS strategy for a given model type and compute budget is largely independent of task difficulty, simplifying the selection process for practitioners.
originalProblem: A systematic and large-scale comparison of various Test-Time Scaling (TTS) strategies for LLMs under identical conditions was lacking.
The impact of different LLM architectures, post-training algorithms, and problem difficulties on the effectiveness of TTS strategies remained poorly understood.
Previous research observed "inverse scaling effects," where increased test-time compute could sometimes degrade accuracy, indicating a need for nuanced understanding.
results: Last Finish Search (LFS) was consistently suboptimal compared to Majority Voting (MV), performing best when configured identically to MV.
First Finish Search (FFS) offered substantial token savings (up to ~90%) relative to Majority Voting, with its accuracy trade-offs varying by model type and problem.

A practical decision matrix was formulated, recommending specific TTS strategies (e.g., MV@N for high compute, FFS-1@N for low compute) based on model family (short-horizon, long-horizon, non-reasoning) and available compute budget.
solution: Conducted an extensive empirical study involving over thirty billion token generations across eight open-source LLMs (7B-235B parameters) and four reasoning datasets.
Evaluated API-friendly parallel TTS strategies, including First Finish Search (FFS), Last Finish Search (LFS), and Beam Search, focusing on accuracy versus token consumption.
Categorized LLMs into "short-horizon" or "long-horizon" models based on how trace length impacts accuracy across easy and hard problems, linking behavior to post-training.
paper_summary.summary: This study systematically investigates test-time compute scaling strategies for Large Language Models across diverse architectures and reasoning tasks. The research identifies distinct "reasoning horizons" in models, challenging the notion that more compute always improves performance and providing a practical guide for optimal strategy selection.

title: The Art of Scaling Test-Time Compute for Large Language Models

abstract: We develop task scaling laws and model ladders to predict the individual task performance of pretrained language models (LMs) in the overtrained setting. Standard power laws for language modeling loss cannot accurately model task performance. Therefore, we leverage a two-step prediction approach: (1) use model and data size to predict an intermediate loss, then (2) use it to predict task performance. We train a set of small-scale "ladder" models, collect data points to fit the parameterized functions of the two prediction steps, and make predictions for two target models: a 7B model trained to 4T tokens and a 13B model trained to 5T tokens. Training the ladder models only costs 1% of the compute used for the target models. On four multiple-choice tasks formatted as ranked classification, we can predict the accuracy of both target models within 2 points of absolute error. We find that tasks with higher prediction error also have higher variance in the metrics over model checkpoints. We also contrast multiple design choices for predicting accuracy, and present recommendations for extending our method to new models and tasks.

keyInsights: The two-step approach, leveraging an intermediate task-specific loss (bits-per-byte), significantly outperforms single-step direct predictions of accuracy from model size and data.
The predictability of a task strongly correlates with its internal variance; tasks exhibiting lower variance across training checkpoints are more accurately predicted.
Within a fixed compute budget for ladder models, increasing model size (N) in the ladder yields greater improvements in prediction accuracy than increasing training tokens (D).
originalProblem: The prohibitive computational costs and time required for training large language models (LMs) make extensive experimentation impractical.
A lack of compute-efficient methods to predict individual downstream task performance for LMs, especially in the common 'overtrained' regime.
Existing scaling laws primarily focused on language modeling loss or required substantial computational resources, failing to address task-specific accuracy efficiently.
results: The method predicted the accuracy of 7B and 13B OLMo 2 target models within 2 points of absolute error on MMLU, HellaSwag, PIQA, and Social IQa.
An average absolute prediction error of approximately 4 points was achieved across all eight tasks and both target models.
The entire process, including training all ladder models, required less than 1% of the total compute needed for the two target OLMo 2 models combined.
solution: A two-step prediction framework that uses a 'model ladder' of 16 small models (190M to 1.3B parameters, trained 1x-10x Chinchilla-optimal data) as the foundation.
Step 1 maps model size (N) and training data (D) to an intermediate task-specific loss (bits-per-byte) using a power law function fitted to the ladder models' final performance.
Step 2 maps the predicted intermediate loss to the final task accuracy using a sigmoidal function, fitted to all intermediate and final checkpoints of the ladder models.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from the Allen Institute for AI, University of Washington, and Princeton University developed a compute-efficient method to predict the individual downstream task performance of large language models, specifically in the overtrained setting. Their two-step approach, using a small set of 'ladder models' and predicting intermediate task loss, accurately forecasted the accuracy of 7B and 13B models within 2 points of absolute error on four out of eight tasks while using less than 1% of the target models' compute.

title: Establishing Task Scaling Laws via Compute-Efficient Model Ladders

abstract: While scaling laws for Large Language Models (LLMs) traditionally focus on proxy metrics like pretraining loss, predicting downstream task performance has been considered unreliable. This paper challenges that view by proposing a direct framework to model the scaling of benchmark performance from the training budget. We find that for a fixed token-to-parameter ratio, a simple power law can accurately describe the scaling behavior of log accuracy on multiple popular downstream tasks. Our results show that the direct approach extrapolates better than the previously proposed two-stage procedure, which is prone to compounding errors. Furthermore, we introduce functional forms that predict accuracy across token-to-parameter ratios and account for inference compute under repeated sampling. We validate our findings on models with up to 17B parameters trained on up to 350B tokens across two dataset mixtures. To support reproducibility and encourage future research, we release the complete set of pretraining losses and downstream evaluation results.

keyInsights: Downstream model performance on diverse benchmarks can be predictably and accurately modeled directly from the training budget, contrary to previous assumptions of unreliability.
Direct scaling laws consistently outperform two-stage, proxy-based methods in extrapolation performance due to the avoidance of compounding errors.
The proposed scaling trends are robust across different data mixtures, although the specific data composition influences the absolute performance levels achieved.
originalProblem: Reliably predicting Large Language Model (LLM) performance on diverse downstream tasks from training budgets has been considered noisy and unreliable.
Existing methods for forecasting downstream performance typically rely on two-stage procedures, which introduce compounding errors and reduce predictive accuracy.
There is a need for simpler, more accurate tools to plan large-scale LLM training runs and understand how capabilities evolve with increased resources.
results: The direct power law for negative log accuracy achieved a mean relative error (MRE) of 1.963% and a mean absolute error (MAE) of 0.015 on validation sets, significantly outperforming two-stage linear and logistic models (MREs of 6.667% and 6.351% respectively).
The extended scaling law accurately captured downstream performance across a range of model parameters and dataset sizes, validating its generalizability for varying token-to-parameter ratios.
A derived functional form successfully modeled pass@k rates for coding tasks, providing a tool to predict performance based on training budget and the number of samples (k).
solution: The authors proposed and validated a direct scaling law for downstream metrics, specifically a power law for negative log accuracy, challenging the need for proxy metrics.
An extended scaling law was developed to model downstream performance across varying token-to-parameter ratios by accounting for both model parameters and dataset size.
A functional form was derived to predict pass@k rates for coding benchmarks like HumanEval, considering training budget and the number of repeated samples (k).
paper_summary.summary: Apple researchers introduced a direct scaling law to predictably model Large Language Model performance on downstream tasks, demonstrating improved accuracy over traditional two-stage methods. This framework offers a simpler and more reliable approach for forecasting LLM capabilities from training budgets, validated across various benchmarks and data mixtures.

title: Revisiting the Scaling Properties of Downstream Metrics in Large Language Model Training

abstract: Test-time scaling improves the reasoning capabilities of large language models (LLMs) by allocating extra compute to generate longer Chains-of-Thoughts (CoTs). This enables models to tackle more complex problem by breaking them down into additional steps, backtracking, and correcting mistakes. Despite its strong performance--demonstrated by OpenAI's o1 and DeepSeek R1, the conditions in the training data under which long CoTs emerge, and when such long CoTs improve the performance, remain unclear. In this paper, we study the performance of test-time scaling for transformers trained on an in-context weight prediction task for linear regression. Our analysis provides a theoretical explanation for several intriguing observations: First, at any fixed test error, increasing test-time compute allows us to reduce the number of in-context examples (context length) in training prompts. Second, if the skills required to solve a downstream task are not sufficiently present in the training data, increasing test-time compute can harm performance. Finally, we characterize task hardness via the smallest eigenvalue of its feature covariance matrix and show that training on a diverse, relevant, and hard set of tasks results in best performance for test-time scaling. We confirm our findings with experiments on large, nonlinear transformer architectures.

keyInsights: Test-time Chain-of-Thought reasoning can be mechanistically understood as a multi-step (pseudo-)Newton's method for minimizing the underlying task loss.
Increasing test-time compute allows for a reduction in the required training context length, highlighting a trade-off between inference-time reasoning and training data density.

"Overthinking" occurs when the foundational skills required by a task are insufficiently represented in the training data, causing additional reasoning steps to amplify errors rather than improve performance.
originalProblem: Lack of theoretical understanding for why test-time scaling (e.g., Chain-of-Thought) improves LLM performance and how training data properties contribute to this.
Unclear conditions under which increasing test-time compute might degrade performance, a phenomenon known as "overthinking."
Absence of rigorous definitions for "difficult" training examples and principles for optimal task selection during training to maximize CoT benefits.
results: Empirical validation on both Linear Self-Attention models and a GPT-2 architecture confirmed that more CoT steps at test time reduce the training context length needed for similar test error.
Experiments demonstrated that when training data is skewed or lacks diverse skills, longer CoT steps at test time can significantly increase test loss, confirming the overthinking phenomenon.

The proposed optimal task selection strategy, derived from the theory, empirically assigns higher training probabilities to harder and more diverse tasks, aligning with theoretical predictions for maximizing test-time scaling benefits.
solution: Analyzed a simplified model: a linear self-attention transformer trained on an in-context weight prediction task for linear regression, enabling rigorous mathematical analysis.
Interpreted test-time Chain-of-Thought (CoT) as a multi-step (pseudo-)Newton's method, providing an algorithmic understanding of its iterative refinement process.
Extended the framework to a multi-task training setting to derive principles for optimal task selection, which were then validated empirically on both linear and nonlinear transformer models.
paper_summary.summary: This research provides a theoretical framework explaining how training data characteristics influence the effectiveness of test-time scaling and Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning in Large Language Models. It defines task hardness and identifies optimal data selection strategies, demonstrating that CoT functions as a multi-step Newton's method and elucidating when increased test-time compute can lead to "overthinking."

title: Understanding the Role of Training Data in Test-Time Scaling

abstract: While scaling laws guide compute allocation for LLM pre-training, analogous prescriptions for reinforcement learning (RL) post-training of large language models (LLMs) remain poorly understood. We study the compute-optimal allocation of sampling compute for on-policy RL methods in LLMs, framing scaling as a compute-constrained optimization over three resources: parallel rollouts per problem, number of problems per batch, and number of update steps. We find that the compute-optimal number of parallel rollouts per problem increases predictably with compute budget and then saturates. This trend holds across both easy and hard problems, though driven by different mechanisms: solution sharpening on easy problems and coverage expansion on hard problems. We further show that increasing the number of parallel rollouts mitigates interference across problems, while the number of problems per batch primarily affects training stability and can be chosen within a broad range. Validated across base models and data distributions, our results recast RL scaling laws as prescriptive allocation rules and provide practical guidance for compute-efficient LLM RL post-training.

keyInsights: RL training stability and effectiveness of regularization are highly sensitive to problem difficulty; easy problems benefit from KL and entropy regularization, while hard problems perform better with these removed.
Square-root scaling of the learning rate with the total effective batch size ($B = B_p \cdot n$) provides the most stable and efficient convergence across a wide range of batch sizes.

The observation that increasing parallel samples ($n$) is beneficial, particularly at higher compute, is attributed to mitigating 'interference across problems' during joint training, which leads to more uniform and efficient gradient updates.
originalProblem: A lack of clear, prescriptive guidelines exists for compute allocation in large language model (LLM) reinforcement learning (RL) post-training, unlike well-established scaling laws in pre-training.
Practitioners struggle to optimally distribute a fixed compute budget across hyperparameters such as parallel rollouts per problem ($n$), problems per batch ($B_p$), and total update steps ($M$).
The inherent complexities of RL, involving tight coupling between data collection and optimization, render its scaling behavior less understood than other AI paradigms.
results: The compute-optimal number of parallel rollouts per problem ($n$) consistently increases with the total sampling compute budget ($C$) before eventually saturating, a trend that generalizes across base models and RL algorithms.
For easy problems, larger $n$ primarily improved "sharpening" (robustness), while for hard problems, it was more critical for improving "coverage" (discovery of successful trajectories).

In joint optimization of all three hyperparameters, the optimal solution was primarily governed by $n$, with $B_p$ largely serving as a stability knob and having marginal impact on performance within a moderate range.
solution: The study conducted extensive empirical experiments, utilizing approximately 120,000 H200-hours of compute, to systematically investigate resource allocation across $B_p$, $n$, and $M$.

A "healthy RL recipe" was established by identifying optimal regularization strategies (difficulty-dependent) and a robust learning rate scaling rule (square-root scaling) to ensure stable training dynamics.

The problem was formulated as a compute-constrained optimization to identify the optimal configuration of ($B_p, n, M$) that maximizes a scalar performance metric for a given total sampling compute budget ($C = B_p \cdot n \cdot M$).
paper_summary.summary: This research develops empirical scaling laws for the reinforcement learning post-training of large language models, providing prescriptive rules for compute-optimal resource allocation. It demonstrates that the ideal number of parallel rollouts per problem increases and then saturates with higher compute budgets, improving robustness on easy tasks and coverage on hard tasks.

title: IsoCompute Playbook: Optimally Scaling Sampling Compute for LLM RL

abstract: Scaling the number of parameters and the size of training data has proven to be an effective strategy for improving large language model (LLM) performance. Yet, as these models grow increasingly powerful and widely deployed, the cost of inference has become a pressing concern. Despite its importance, the trade-off between model accuracy and inference efficiency remains underexplored. In this work, we examine how key architectural factors, hidden size, the allocation of parameters between MLP and attention (mlp-to-attention ratio), and grouped-query attention (GQA), influence both inference cost and accuracy. We introduce a conditional scaling law that augments the Chinchilla framework with architectural information, along with a search framework for identifying architectures that are simultaneously inference-efficient and accurate. To validate our approach, we train more than 200 models spanning 80M to 3B parameters and 8B to 100B training tokens, and fit the proposed conditional scaling law. Our results show that the conditional scaling law reliably predicts optimal architectural choices and that the resulting models outperform existing open-source baselines. Under the same training budget, optimized architectures achieve up to 2.1% higher accuracy and 42% greater inference throughput compared to LLaMA-3.2.
title: Scaling Laws Meet Model Architecture: Toward Inference-Efficient LLMs

abstract:
Large language model (LLM) scaling laws are empirical formulas that estimate
changes in model quality as a result of increasing parameter count and training
data. However, these formulas, including the popular Deepmind Chinchilla
scaling laws, neglect to include the cost of inference. We modify the
Chinchilla scaling laws to calculate the optimal LLM parameter count and
pre-training data size to train and deploy a model of a given quality and
inference demand. We conduct our analysis both in terms of a compute budget and
real-world costs and find that LLM researchers expecting reasonably large
inference demand (~1B requests) should train models smaller and longer than
Chinchilla-optimal. Furthermore, we train 47 models of varying sizes and
parameter counts to validate our formula and find that model quality continues
to improve as we scale tokens per parameter to extreme ranges (up to 10,000).
Finally, we ablate the procedure used to fit the Chinchilla scaling law
coefficients and find that developing scaling laws only from data collected at
typical token/parameter ratios overestimates the impact of additional tokens at
these extreme ranges.

keyInsights: Optimizing for total lifetime cost (training + inference) shifts the optimal LLM design towards smaller models trained on substantially more data than suggested by training-only scaling laws.

Empirical results indicate no saturation in model quality, with pre-training loss and downstream task performance consistently improving even at very high token-to-parameter ratios (e.g., 10,000 for 150M models).

The coefficients of existing scaling laws change and become "flatter" when fitted to data covering extreme token-to-parameter ratios, suggesting limitations in their extrapolation to very long training durations.
originalProblem: Existing LLM scaling laws focus solely on optimizing training compute, overlooking the often dominant and cumulative costs of inference.
Developers lacked quantitative guidance for balancing training effort (model size vs. data amount) with anticipated inference demand to minimize total lifecycle cost.
The behavior of LLMs at extremely high token-to-parameter ratios was not empirically validated, questioning the generalizability of current scaling laws to longer training durations.
results: Incorporating inference costs can lead to significant total cost savings, with calculations showing reductions of over 50% for high-inference scenarios by training smaller models more extensively.
For example, a 70B-Chinchilla-quality model with high inference demand suggests a 21.5B parameter model trained on 27 trillion tokens, yielding 54% cost savings.

Empirical training runs confirmed that models learn with similar efficiency across a wide range of token-per-parameter ratios (up to 500) and that quality continues to improve even at 10,000 tokens/parameter.
solution: The Chinchilla scaling law framework was modified to include total lifetime computational and monetary costs, encompassing both training and inference FLOPs.
An optimization problem was formulated and solved computationally to determine optimal model parameters and training data size for a target loss, given inference demand.

Extensive empirical validation involved training 47 transformer models (150M-6B parameters) across token-per-parameter ratios up to 10,000 to observe loss and downstream performance trends at extreme data scales.
paper_summary.summary: Research from Databricks MosaicML introduces an extended framework for LLM scaling laws that incorporates both pre-training and inference costs, demonstrating that training smaller models on significantly more data yields substantial total cost reductions (e.g., over 50% for certain configurations) compared to training-only optimal approaches. Empirical findings also show continued model quality improvement at very high token-to-parameter ratios, up to 10,000 tokens per parameter.

title: Beyond Chinchilla-Optimal: Accounting for Inference in Language Model  Scaling Laws

abstract: Scaling laws in language modeling traditionally quantify training loss as a function of dataset size and model parameters, providing compute-optimal estimates but often neglecting the impact of data quality on model generalization. In this paper, we extend the conventional understanding of scaling law by offering a microscopic view of data quality within the original formulation -- effective training tokens -- which we posit to be a critical determinant of performance for parameter-constrained language models. Specifically, we formulate the proposed term of effective training tokens to be a combination of two readily-computed indicators of text: (i) text diversity and (ii) syntheticity as measured by a teacher model. We pretrained over $200$ models of 25M to 1.5B parameters on a diverse set of sampled, synthetic data, and estimated the constants that relate text quality, model size, training tokens, and eight reasoning task accuracy scores. We demonstrated the estimated constants yield +0.83 Pearson correlation with true accuracies, and analyzed it in scenarios involving widely-used data techniques such as data sampling and synthesis which aim to improve data quality.

keyInsights: Data quality, measured by diversity and syntheticity, has a significant impact on language model performance, particularly for smaller models (25M-500M parameters).
The benefit of data quality varies with model size: smaller models benefit more from increased syntheticity (e.g., via text synthesis), while larger models gain more from data quantity and diversity.
Data refinement techniques, especially text synthesis, can substantially increase the 'effective token' count, which accounts for both data quantity and quality.
originalProblem: Traditional language model scaling laws primarily consider model size, dataset size, and computational resources, often overlooking the critical role of data quality.
There was a lack of understanding regarding how data quality influences the performance of parameter-constrained language models, which are crucial for efficient or on-device applications.
Existing frameworks did not adequately quantify the combined effect of data quantity and quality on model performance predictions.
results: A revised scaling law incorporating a 'quality scaling factor' (Q) achieved a strong Pearson correlation of +0.83 with true model accuracies.

For models up to 500M parameters, data refinement through text synthesis demonstrated the most significant improvements in performance by increasing 'effective training tokens' compared to data selection or original data.

Smaller models (25M-500M) showed greater sensitivity to data quality improvements, with synthetic data (higher syntheticity, lower diversity) proving more beneficial for them, while larger models benefited more from overall data quantity.
solution: The study extended traditional scaling laws by introducing a 'quality scaling factor' (Q) into the formulation, defining 'effective training tokens' as a product of data quantity and quality.
Data quality was quantified using two primary components: diversity (measured by compression ratio) and syntheticity (estimated by perplexity from a teacher model).
Over 200 language models, ranging from 25M to 1.5B parameters, were trained on original, selected, and synthetic datasets to empirically validate the impact of data quality on performance.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from Meta AI and academic institutions demonstrated that for parameter-constrained language models (25M to 1.5B), data quality can be as or more important than data quantity, by incorporating a quality scaling factor into revised scaling laws. The study found that text synthesis significantly improved 'effective training tokens' and model performance, particularly for smaller models.

title: Scaling Parameter-Constrained Language Models with Quality Data

abstract:
Test-Time Scaling (TTS) improves the reasoning performance of Large Language
Models (LLMs) by allocating additional compute during inference. We conduct a
structured survey of TTS methods and categorize them into sampling-based,
search-based, and trajectory optimization strategies. We observe that
reasoning-optimized models often produce less diverse outputs, which limits TTS
effectiveness. To address this, we propose ADAPT (A Diversity Aware Prefix
fine-Tuning), a lightweight method that applies prefix tuning with a
diversity-focused data strategy. Experiments on mathematical reasoning tasks
show that ADAPT reaches 80% accuracy using eight times less compute than strong
baselines. Our findings highlight the essential role of generative diversity in
maximizing TTS effectiveness.

keyInsights: Generative diversity is a critical factor for the effectiveness of Test-Time Scaling (TTS) methods in Large Language Models.
Reasoning-optimized models often exhibit reduced generative diversity, which limits the gains achievable through sampling-based TTS.
Enhancing diversity in the initial reasoning steps can significantly improve TTS efficiency while preserving core reasoning capabilities.
originalProblem: Large Language Models (LLMs) incur high computational costs for training and inference, posing barriers to their widespread adoption.
While Test-Time Scaling (TTS) improves performance by allocating more compute during inference, its effectiveness is limited by a lack of generative diversity in specialized models.
Reasoning-optimized LLMs, despite their accuracy, produce less varied outputs, hindering the exploration of diverse solution paths that TTS methods rely on.
results: ADAPT achieved 80% accuracy with 32 samples, demonstrating an 8x improvement in compute efficiency compared to the baseline DeepSeek model, which required 256 samples for similar performance.
The method preserved the strong baseline performance of the reasoning-optimized model (65.2% at N=2) while significantly enhancing its ability to scale with additional compute.
ADAPT-enhanced models combined high baseline accuracy with robust gains from increased Test-Time Scaling, validating the hypothesis that diversity is crucial for TTS effectiveness.
solution: Researchers from National Taiwan University propose ADAPT (A Diversity-Aware Prefix Fine-Tuning), a lightweight method to enhance early-stage output diversity.
ADAPT selectively fine-tunes only the first 512 tokens of reasoning trajectories to introduce diversity without compromising the model's core reasoning abilities.
The method trains on a curated dataset comprising 90% diverse responses from a general model and 10% target model outputs to balance diversity injection with quality preservation.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers at National Taiwan University identified that generative diversity is a critical, underexplored factor in the effectiveness of Test-Time Scaling (TTS) for LLMs. They developed ADAPT, a method that enhances early-stage output diversity in reasoning-optimized models, leading to an 8x improvement in compute efficiency for achieving 80% accuracy on mathematical reasoning tasks.

title: Revisiting Test-Time Scaling: A Survey and a Diversity-Aware Method for  Efficient Reasoning

abstract:
Scaling laws are useful guides for derisking expensive training runs, as they
predict performance of large models using cheaper, small-scale experiments.
However, there remain gaps between current scaling studies and how language
models are ultimately trained and evaluated. For instance, scaling is usually
studied in the compute-optimal training regime (i.e., "Chinchilla optimal"
regime). In contrast, models are often over-trained to reduce inference costs.
Moreover, scaling laws mostly predict loss on next-token prediction, but models
are usually compared on downstream task performance. To address both
shortcomings, we create a testbed of 104 models with 0.011B to 6.9B parameters
trained with various numbers of tokens on three data distributions. First, we
fit scaling laws that extrapolate in both the amount of over-training and the
number of model parameters. This enables us to predict the validation loss of a
1.4B parameter, 900B token run (i.e., 32$\times$ over-trained) and a 6.9B
parameter, 138B token run (i.e., a compute-optimal run)$\unicode{x2014}$each
from experiments that take 300$\times$ less compute. Second, we relate the
perplexity of a language model to its downstream task performance by proposing
a power law. We use this law to predict top-1 error averaged over downstream
tasks for the two aforementioned models, using experiments that take 20$\times$
less compute. Our experiments are available at
https://github.com/mlfoundations/scaling.

author_info.institution: The University of Texas at Austin

title:
Language models scale reliably with over-training and on downstream
  tasks

abstract: The quality of Large Language Model (LLM) pretraining depends on multiple factors, including the compute budget and the choice of optimization algorithm. Empirical scaling laws are widely used to predict loss as model size and training data grow, however, almost all existing studies fix the optimizer (typically AdamW). At the same time, a new generation of optimizers (e.g., Muon, Shampoo, SOAP) promises faster and more stable convergence, but their relationship with model and data scaling is not yet well understood. In this work, we study scaling laws across different optimizers. Empirically, we show that 1) separate Chinchilla-style scaling laws for each optimizer are ill-conditioned and have highly correlated parameters. Instead, 2) we propose a more robust law with shared power-law exponents and optimizer-specific rescaling factors, which enable direct comparison between optimizers. Finally, 3) we provide a theoretical analysis of gradient-based methods for the proxy task of a convex quadratic objective, demonstrating that Chinchilla-style scaling laws emerge naturally as a result of loss decomposition into irreducible, approximation, and optimization errors.
title: Towards Robust Scaling Laws for Optimizers

abstract: While scaling laws provide a reliable methodology for predicting train loss across compute scales for a single data distribution, less is known about how these predictions should change as we change the distribution. In this paper, we derive a strategy for predicting one loss from another and apply it to predict across different pre-training datasets and from pre-training data to downstream task data. Our predictions extrapolate well even at 20x the largest FLOP budget used to fit the curves. More precisely, we find that there are simple shifted power law relationships between (1) the train losses of two models trained on two separate datasets when the models are paired by training compute (train-to-train), (2) the train loss and the test loss on any downstream distribution for a single model (train-to-test), and (3) the test losses of two models trained on two separate train datasets (test-to-test). The results hold up for pre-training datasets that differ substantially (some are entirely code and others have no code at all) and across a variety of downstream tasks. Finally, we find that in some settings these shifted power law relationships can yield more accurate predictions than extrapolating single-dataset scaling laws.

keyInsights: Shifted power law relationships consistently describe empirical trends for model loss across various pre-training datasets and for transfer to downstream tasks, extrapolating effectively to significantly larger models.

The chosen scaling law parameterization for individual models is compatible with loss translation, implying that the compute-optimal model size for a given FLOP budget may remain invariant across different data distributions where the relationship holds.

Cross-entropy loss serves as a stable and smooth metric for predicting model performance and tracking progress, avoiding the 'emergent' behaviors often observed with accuracy metrics on downstream tasks.
originalProblem: Existing scaling laws for large language models are distribution-dependent, limiting their application when training or evaluation data changes.
There is a lack of understanding regarding how model performance predictability shifts when transitioning between diverse pre-training datasets or from pre-training to downstream evaluation tasks.
Extensive computational resources are often required to fit new scaling laws for every novel data distribution, hindering efficient model development.
results: Loss-to-loss fits for new datasets, using only 8 models and a source dataset, achieved R-squared values (0.986-0.991) comparable to those from full 'skyline' fits (0.987-0.992) using 88 models, significantly outperforming independent scaling laws fit with limited data (0.450-0.975).

The methodology accurately predicted the test loss of large models on new datasets with relative errors between 1.2% and 17.6%, generally surpassing baselines like FLOPs-to-test, independent scaling laws, and identity prediction.

Train-to-train predictions showed exponents (kappa) near 1 (0.88-1.13) indicating similar scaling, while train-to-test relationships for downstream tasks often exhibited concave curves (kappa < 1), suggesting increasing returns for improvements in training loss.
solution: The paper introduces 'loss-to-loss prediction,' a methodology that posits relationships between losses on different data distributions can be described by simple shifted power laws.
A general functional form, L_i(f_N,D_j) 	 K 	 (L_k(f_N,D_l) - E_k|l)^ + E_i|j, is proposed and validated across train-to-train, train-to-test, and test-to-test scenarios.
A suite of 528 transformer models, ranging from 20 million to 1.7 billion parameters, was pre-trained on six diverse datasets to empirically test the proposed loss-to-loss relationships.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers at Harvard's Kempner Institute developed a 'loss-to-loss prediction' methodology that consistently describes how model loss translates across different data distributions using simple shifted power laws. This approach enables the translation of scaling laws to new datasets with minimal additional training and more accurate prediction of large model performance, outperforming traditional baselines.

title: Loss-to-Loss Prediction: Scaling Laws for All Datasets

abstract:
State-of-the-art LLMs are powered by scaling -- scaling model size, dataset
size and cluster size. It is economically infeasible to extensively tune
hyperparameter for the largest runs. Instead, approximately optimal
hyperparameters must be inferred or \textit{transferred} from smaller
experiments. Hyperparameter transfer across model sizes has been studied in
Yang et al. However, hyperparameter transfer across dataset size -- or token
horizon -- has not been studied yet. To remedy this we conduct a large scale
empirical study on how optimal learning rate (LR) depends on token horizon in
LLM training. We first demonstrate that the optimal LR changes significantly
with token horizon -- longer training necessitates smaller LR. Secondly we
demonstrate the the optimal LR follows a scaling law, and that the optimal LR
for longer horizons can be accurately estimated from shorter horizons via such
scaling laws. We also provide a rule-of-thumb for transferring LR across token
horizons with zero overhead over current practices. Lastly we provide evidence
that LLama-1 used too high LR, and estimate the performance hit from this. We
thus argue that hyperparameter transfer across data size is an important and
overlooked component of LLM training.
title: Scaling Optimal LR Across Token Horizons

abstract: We study empirical scaling laws for language model performance on the cross-entropy loss. The loss scales as a power-law with model size, dataset size, and the amount of compute used for training, with some trends spanning more than seven orders of magnitude. Other architectural details such as network width or depth have minimal effects within a wide range. Simple equations govern the dependence of overfitting on model/dataset size and the dependence of training speed on model size. These relationships allow us to determine the optimal allocation of a fixed compute budget. Larger models are significantly more sample-efficient, such that optimally compute-efficient training involves training very large models on a relatively modest amount of data and stopping significantly before convergence.

keyInsights: Language model performance (loss) scales as precise power-laws with model size (N), dataset size (D), and optimal training compute (C_min), exhibiting no deviation at the tested upper scales.

Architectural details like network width or depth have minimal effect on performance when the total number of non-embedding parameters (N) is fixed, emphasizing the importance of scale over specific 'shape'.
Optimal compute allocation prioritizes training very large models on relatively modest data, stopping before full convergence, with most compute dedicated to increasing model size (N \u221d C^0.73).
originalProblem: Understanding the precise dependence of language model performance on architectural choices, model size, computational power, and data quantity.
Moving beyond anecdotal observations to a scientific, empirical understanding of why deep learning models perform well and how to improve them efficiently.
Optimizing the allocation of increasingly expensive computational resources for large neural networks.
results: Language model loss was found to scale as N^(-0.076) for model size, D^(-0.095) for dataset size, and C_min^(-0.050) for optimal compute.
Larger models demonstrate greater sample efficiency, achieving equivalent performance with fewer optimization steps and less data than smaller models.
The findings provided a quantitative roadmap for large language model development, directly informing the design and training strategies of models like GPT-3.
solution: Conducted extensive empirical experiments across vast scales using Transformer models, systematically varying model size (N), dataset size (D), and training compute (C).
Measured cross-entropy loss as the primary performance metric, fitting observed data to power-law equations to derive scaling relationships.
Developed a methodology to account for optimal batch size, allowing for accurate prediction of minimum compute (C_min) required for a given loss level.
paper_summary.summary: An empirical study at OpenAI investigated how neural language model performance scales with model size, dataset size, and training compute, revealing precise power-law relationships that govern cross-entropy loss over many orders of magnitude. The research establishes a quantitative framework for predicting performance and optimizing resource allocation in large language model development.

title: Scaling Laws for Neural Language Models

abstract: Scaling laws are powerful tools to predict the performance of large language models. However, current scaling laws fall short of accounting for inference costs. In this work, we first show that model architecture affects inference latency, where models of the same size can have up to 3.5x difference in latency. To tackle this challenge, we modify the Chinchilla scaling laws to co-optimize the model parameter count, the number of training tokens, and the model architecture. Due to the reason that models of similar training loss exhibit gaps in downstream evaluation, we also propose a novel method to train inference-efficient models based on the revised scaling laws. We perform extensive empirical studies to fit and evaluate our inference-aware scaling laws. We vary model parameters from 80M to 1B, training tokens from 1.6B to 30B, and model shapes, training 63 models. Guided by our inference-efficient scaling law and model selection method, we release the Morph-1B model, which improves inference latency by 1.8x while maintaining accuracy on downstream tasks compared to open-source models, pushing the Pareto frontier of accuracy-latency tradeoff. Notably, our experiments reveal that wider and shallower models can yield efficiency gains while preserving accuracy.

keyInsights: Model architecture, specifically the ratio of hidden size to layers (aspect ratio), plays a critical role in inference efficiency, with wider and shallower models demonstrating significantly lower latency for a given parameter count.

The proposed inference-efficient scaling law substantially improves the prediction of model loss and, more importantly, the ranking of model performance compared to traditional scaling laws, especially in the context of inference-aware design.
Data from "over-trained" models (trained beyond Chinchilla-optimal recommendations) is essential for fitting robust and accurate inference-aware scaling laws, improving their generalizability.
originalProblem: Existing LLM scaling laws primarily focus on training costs and performance, overlooking the dominant long-term inference costs in real-world applications.

Current scaling laws fail to account for how model architecture, specifically the ratio of hidden size to layers, significantly impacts inference latency and throughput, independent of total parameter count.
The traditional constraint of fixed training FLOPs is impractical, as real-world LLM development is more often limited by deployment device memory and data availability.
results: The developed Morph-1B model, guided by the new scaling law, demonstrated a 1.8x improvement in inference latency while maintaining accuracy comparable to existing open-source models of similar size.

The inference-efficient scaling law achieved superior predictive accuracy, reducing Mean Squared Error for loss prediction from 0.0033 to 0.0006 and improving R² from 0.9895 to 0.9982 over the Chinchilla law.

The new scaling law significantly improved the accuracy of ranking model performance, with Spearman's rank correlation coefficient reaching 1.00 for 1B models, compared to -0.40 for the Chinchilla law.
solution: Developed an "inference-efficient scaling law" that extends the Chinchilla law by incorporating model architecture (aspect ratio R = d_model / n_layers) to predict training loss more accurately for various model shapes.

Conducted extensive empirical studies, training 63 transformer models with varied parameters, training data, and aspect ratios, to fit and validate the new scaling law and profile inference efficiency.

Proposed a multi-step methodology for model selection that uses the new scaling law to predict loss and rank candidate models, balancing inference efficiency with predicted downstream task performance before full evaluation.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers at the University of Wisconsin-Madison extended existing language model scaling laws to incorporate model architecture and inference efficiency, developing a new law that predicts loss based on parameters, training data, and the ratio of hidden size to layers. This new framework guided the development of Morph-1B, an inference-efficient model achieving 1.8x faster inference latency with accuracy comparable to larger existing models.

title: Scaling Inference-Efficient Language Models

abstract: Scaling large language models (LLMs) has driven significant advancements, yet it faces diminishing returns and escalating energy demands. This work explores how test-time compute (TTC) can serve as an energy-efficient complement to conventional scaling strategies by allocating additional computational resources at inference time rather than during training. Specifically, we investigate whether employing TTC can achieve superior accuracy-energy trade-offs compared to simply increasing model size. Our empirical analysis reveals that TTC surpasses traditional model scaling in accuracy/energy efficiency, with notable gains in tasks demanding complex reasoning rather than mere factual recall. Further, we identify a critical interaction between TTC performance and output sequence length, demonstrating that strategically adjusting compute resources at inference time according to query complexity can substantially enhance efficiency. Our findings advocate for TTC as a promising direction, enabling more sustainable, accurate, and adaptable deployment of future language models.
title: The Energy Cost of Reasoning: Analyzing Energy Usage in LLMs with Test-time Compute

abstract:
Scaling laws guide the development of large language models (LLMs) by
offering estimates for the optimal balance of model size, tokens, and compute.
More recently, loss-to-loss scaling laws that relate losses across pretraining
datasets and downstream tasks have emerged as a powerful tool for understanding
and improving LLM performance. In this work, we investigate which factors most
strongly influence loss-to-loss scaling. Our experiments reveal that the
pretraining data and tokenizer determine the scaling trend. In contrast, model
size, optimization hyperparameters, and even significant architectural
differences, such as between transformer-based models like Llama and
state-space models like Mamba, have limited impact. Consequently, practitioners
should carefully curate suitable pretraining datasets for optimal downstream
performance, while architectures and other settings can be freely optimized for
training efficiency.

organization_info.name: Max Planck Institute for Intelligent Systems

keyInsights: Loss-to-loss scaling laws, which characterize the relationship between pretraining and downstream performance, consistently follow shifted power law functions across diverse LLM configurations.
The choice and characteristics of the pretraining data are the most significant factors influencing the shape and position of loss-to-loss scaling curves, directly dictating generalization behavior.

Architectural differences (e.g., Transformers vs. State-Space Models), tokenizer choice, model size, context length, and optimization settings have minimal to no causal impact on loss-to-loss scaling.
originalProblem: Traditional scaling laws predict pretraining performance but do not directly explain or predict a model's performance on diverse downstream tasks.
A systematic understanding of how various design choices, such as pretraining data, architecture, and training configurations, causally affect loss-to-loss scaling laws was lacking.
Prior work on loss-to-loss relationships was largely confined to single architectural or training setups, limiting insights into broader generalization determinants.
results: Empirical data strongly confirmed shifted power law scaling, with high coefficients of determination (R 2 values near 0.999) across various validation and test sets.
Changing pretraining datasets (e.g., FineWeb-Edu, C4, The Pile) resulted in significant and distinct shifts in loss-to-loss scaling curves.

Architectural variations (Llama vs. Mamba), tokenizer choices, model size (59M to 420M parameters), context length, and various optimization settings produced only negligible changes to the loss-to-loss scaling curves.
solution: Conducted a systematic interventional analysis by training and evaluating over 6000 Large Language Model checkpoints (including Llama and Mamba architectures).
Modeled loss-to-loss relationships using a shifted power law, normalizing losses by bits-per-byte (BPB) for fair comparison across different tokenizers and models.
Systematically varied key factors like pretraining data, model architecture, tokenizer, model size, context length, and optimization settings to observe their causal impact on scaling curves.
paper_summary.summary: A study found that pretraining data is the primary determinant of loss-to-loss scaling laws in Large Language Models, which consistently follow shifted power laws, while factors like architecture, tokenizer, model size, context length, and optimization settings have negligible influence on these generalization trends.

title: LLMs on the Line: Data Determines Loss-to-Loss Scaling Laws

abstract: Scaling training compute, measured in FLOPs, has long been shown to improve the accuracy of large language models, yet training remains resource-intensive. Prior work shows that increasing test-time compute (TTC)-for example through iterative sampling-can allow smaller models to rival or surpass much larger ones at lower overall cost. We introduce TTC-aware training, where an intermediate checkpoint and a corresponding TTC configuration can together match or exceed the accuracy of a fully trained model while requiring substantially fewer training FLOPs. Building on this insight, we propose an early stopping algorithm that jointly selects a checkpoint and TTC configuration to minimize training compute without sacrificing accuracy. To make this practical, we develop an efficient TTC evaluation method that avoids exhaustive search, and we formalize a break-even bound that identifies when increased inference compute compensates for reduced training compute. Experiments demonstrate up to 92\% reductions in training FLOPs while maintaining and sometimes remarkably improving accuracy. These results highlight a new perspective for balancing training and inference compute in model development, enabling faster deployment cycles and more frequent model refreshes. Codes will be publicly released.
title: FLOP-Efficient Training: Early Stopping Based on Test-Time Compute Awareness

abstract: Neural scaling laws have become foundational for optimizing large language model (LLM) training, yet they typically assume a single dense model output.
